texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We show that the posterior distribution of parameters in a hidden Markov model with parametric emission distributions and discrete and known state space is asymptotically normal. The main novelty of our proof is that it is based on a testing condition and the sequence of test functions is obtained using an optimal transportation inequality.
texts.text: We show that the posterior distribution of parameters in a hidden Markov model with parametric emission distributions and discrete and known state space is asymptotically normal. The main novelty of our proof is that it is based on a testing condition and the sequence of test functions is obtained using an optimal transportation inequality.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Bayesian inference; Bernstein-von Mises theorem; hidden Markov model; transportation inequality.
texts.text: Keywords: Bayesian inference; Bernstein-von Mises theorem; hidden Markov model; transportation inequality.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hidden Markov models with parametric emission distributions and discrete and known state space, which we shorten as HMMs, have been widely used in diverse areas for analyzing dependent discretetime data [11, 10, 26]. Our focus is on studying asymptotic properties of the posterior distribution of parameters in an HMM [4, 5]. We prove a Bernstein-von Mises theorem for HMMs using a testing condition. Informally, this theorem says that the posterior distribution of parameters in an HMM converges in distribution to a normal distribution that centers at the maximum likelihood estimator of parameters, and its covariance matrix equals inverse of the Fisher information matrix.
texts.text: Hidden Markov models with parametric emission distributions and discrete and known state space, which we shorten as HMMs, have been widely used in diverse areas for analyzing dependent discretetime data [11, 10, 26]. Informally, this theorem says that the posterior distribution of parameters in an HMM converges in distribution to a normal distribution that centers at the maximum likelihood estimator of parameters, and its covariance matrix equals inverse of the Fisher information matrix.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider the setup of a general hidden Markov model as a discrete-time stochastic process ( X t , Y t ) t ≥ 1 , where ( Y t ) t ≥ 1 process is observed and ( X t ) t ≥ 1 process is unobserved and Markov. Its state space includes all possible values of ( X t ) t ≥ 1 and emission distribution is defined as the conditional distribution of Y t given X t . Our HMMs are a special case of this setup in that the emission distributions are parametric and state space is discrete and known. There are many theoretical results about frequentist estimation in hidden Markov models with discrete, continuous, or unknown state spaces and parametric or non-parametric emission distributions [1, 22, 2, 18, 7, 8]. The equivalent asymptotic Bayesian results have started to emerge recently [5, 12, 25, 13, 9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our theoretical results are closest to those of De Gunst and Shcherbakova [5]. They prove a Bernstein-von Mises theorem for HMMs using Taylor expansion of the log likelihood function. Our proof differs from theirs in that we adopt the setup of Ghosal et al. [14] for deriving posterior contraction rates in general Bayesian models. Using this setup, we adapt the proof of Theorem 10.1 in [23] to show that the posterior distribution of parameters in an HMM is asymptotically normal. Compared to [5], our assumption on the log likelihood function is milder in that our proof assumes only the first order smoothness of the log likelihood function. More importantly, our proof relies on the local asymptotic normality condition for HMMs, which is satisfied under the assumptions of Bickel et al. [2], De Gunst and Shcherbakova [5]. Our proof techniques are similar to those used for investigating asymptotic properties of the posterior distribution in extensions of HMMs with non-parametric emission distributions [12, 25]. A key step in our proof is to establish a testing condition based on a sequence of test functions. We are not aware of any existing test function that is tuned for such applications in HMMs.

More importantly, our proof relies on the local asymptotic normality condition for HMMs, which is satisfied under the assumptions of Bickel et al. [2], De Gunst and Shcherbakova [5]. Our proof techniques are similar to those used for investigating asymptotic properties of the posterior distribution in extensions of HMMs with non-parametric emission distributions [12, 25]. A key step in our proof is to establish a testing condition based on a sequence of test functions. We are not aware of any existing test function that is tuned for such applications in HMMs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our main contribution is to establish the testing condition using a sequence of test functions obtained from an optimal transportation cost inequality. The testing condition measures the complexity of the HMM family and establishing it is a key step in showing the consistency of posterior distribution [14]. Our construction of the sequence of test functions is based on the L 1 -transportation cost information inequality for HMMs [6, 19] and Lipschitz concentration of log likelihood function of HMMs [20]. Hu [17] has constructed the likelihood ratio test with exponentially decaying error probabilities for testing simple hypotheses. Extending this work, we construct the sequence of test functions with exponentially decaying error probabilities for testing general hypotheses using the L 1 -transportation cost information inequality. The sequence of test functions satisfies the testing condition in [14, 23] and is used to prove the Bernstein-von Mises theorem for HMMs.
texts.text: Our main contribution is to establish the testing condition using a sequence of test functions obtained from an optimal transportation cost inequality. The testing condition measures the complexity of the HMM family and establishing it is a key step in showing the consistency of posterior distribution [14]. Our construction of the sequence of test functions is based on the L 1 -transportation cost information inequality for HMMs [6, 19] and Lipschitz concentration of log likelihood function of HMMs [20]. Hu [17] has constructed the likelihood ratio test with exponentially decaying error probabilities for testing simple hypotheses. Extending this work, we construct the sequence of test functions with exponentially decaying error probabilities for testing general hypotheses using the L 1 -transportation cost information inequality. The sequence of test functions satisfies the testing condition in [14, 23] and is used to prove the Bernstein-von Mises theorem for HMMs.

texts.orig: This paper is structured in four sections. In Section 2, we state the HMM setup, define the prediction filter, and restructure the results of Kontorovich et al. [19] and Hu [17] in the context of this paper. The assumptions and main results about the testing condition are presented in Section 3. The contraction rate and asymptotic normality of the posterior distribution of parameters in an HMM follow immediately from the testing condition. Finally, Section 4 contains proofs of the main results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our main contribution is to construct φ n in an HMM and prove that it satisfies the testing condition in (3.2). Starting from the simple hypothesis, we construct the likelihood ratio test with exponentially decaying error based on [17]. Applying the L 1 -transportation cost information inequality for HMMs, we show that the log likelihood function is a Lipschitz function of the extended HMM sequence ( Y t , p θ t ) n t =1 , and it concentrates on its mean with sub-Gaussian tail behavior. Then, for any ǫ > 0, some 0 < ξ < 1, and any θ 1 with ‖ θ 1 -θ 0 ‖ 2 > ǫ , we consider hypothesis of P ( n ) θ 0 versus the complement { P ( n ) θ : θ ∈ Θ , ‖ θ -θ 1 ‖ 2 ≤ ξǫ } . By taking the liklihood ratio tests of P ( n ) θ 0 versus P n θ ′ where θ ′ is the center of { θ ∈ Θ , ‖ θ -θ 1 ‖ 2 ≤ ξǫ } , the constructed test has an exponential decaying error. Finally, we prove the testing condition (3.2) by an entropy bound of { P ( n ) θ : θ ∈ Θ , jǫ n < ‖ θ -θ 0 ‖ 2 ≤ 2 jǫ n } .

texts.orig: Bickel et al. [2] have developed the first set of results showing the consistency and asymptotic normality of maximum likelihood estimators of the parameters in an HMM. Corollary 3.3 provides similar theoretical gaurantees about Bayesian estimation in HMMs. Specifically, it states that the posterior distribution of θ given the observations Y n 1 in an HMM is asymptotically normal with random mean vector θ 0 + 1 √ n ∆ n, 0 and covariance matrix ( nI θ 0 ) -1 . Compared to the similar results in [5], which are based on Taylor expansion of the log likelihood function, Corollary 3.3 provides an alternative approach to prove the Bernstein-von Mises theorem using the LAN condition (3.8) and testing condition (3.4). The LAN condition is a general regularity assumption on the class of parametric models { P ( n ) θ : θ ∈ Θ } , which is satisfied under the theoretical setup in [2, 5]. In our proof, we construct the sequence of test functions based on a likelihood ratio test and prove the testing condition using the optimal transportation inequality for HMMs.
